Retro Impact Past 3 Months:
We successfully completed all the main activities and deliverables within the 2025-Q4 scope. As a result, we deployed a new version of Scout and developed an AI-based proof of concept for vulnerability detection. This POC is based on auditor-crafted prompting and was able to identify issues from existing audits.

We deployed the new version of Scout in November, and in December we saw a peak of 1,658 crate downloads.

We currently have no metrics for the AI POC. Our strategy for 2026-Q1 is to continue experimenting with AI agents and later evaluate how to scale the POC into an initial product that, together with Scout, would form a new suite of security vulnerability detection tools for Soroban.

Goals Upcoming 3 Months:
Our plan has been developed having two main objectives:
1. Continue adding vulnerability detectors to Scout
2. Experiment AI agents for vulnerability detection

As stated in our Results Document, the work completed in 2025-Q4 has crystallized our path forward. We have successfully engineered a tool for structured prompting and proven that our abstract prompts can detect confirmed vulnerabilities. However, the experimental results show that simply feeding more files into a large context window is insufficient for consistent detection.

Future development will pivot to a research phase designed to solve context saturation. We plan to evaluate an advanced architecture leveraging multi-agent workflows and chained API calls. This architectural evolution is intended to solve the noise issue by intelligently narrowing the scope of analysis before the final vulnerability assessment takes place.

Goals Upcoming 3 Months (from Previous S:
CoinFabrik has been actively researching the application of AI for vulnerability detection and clustering in smart contracts. Our work has shown that AI can complement traditional deterministic analysis by reasoning over patterns, data flows, and complex interactions that rule-based tools may miss, thereby reducing false negatives and providing explainable insights. Integrating these AI techniques with existing static analysis frameworks can significantly enhance the security assessment of Stellar smart contracts, enabling more reliable detection of vulnerabilities, faster audit cycles, and ultimately contributing to the robustness and trustworthiness of applications built on the Stellar network.

We are proposing for 2025-Q4 and following grants to progressively complete 20025-Q3 detectors while also advancing our AI initiative.

Month 1 – Add new detectors - Chunk 1
- Review historical and recent Soroban audit reports.
- Identify vulnerabilities that can be translated into static detection patterns.
- Engage with Stellar Audit Bank and Stellar Development Foundation security team to share insights and validate relevance of potential detectors.
- Design and implement at least 3 new detectors.

Month 2 – AI Research and Planning
- Survey the state of the art in static analysis and AI techniques applied to smart contracts (e.g., LLMs).
- Choose the most appropriate approach for the POC (Proof of Concept) based on the survey results.
- Select an initial set of smart contracts to use as test cases for validating the POC.

Month 3 – AI POC Design and Construction
- Design and build the POC based on the defined approach.
- Run the POC on the initial set of contracts to validate its functionality.
- Run the POC on an expanded set of contracts to evaluate scalability and effectiveness.

Retro Impact Past 3 Months:
Over the last three months, I delivered a major upgrade of Stellar’s hardware wallet infrastructure across both Ledger and Trezor ecosystems.

On the Ledger side, the Stellar Ledger App was fully rewritten in Rust. This significantly reduced architectural complexity, improved user experience, and established a much cleaner and more maintainable codebase for long-term development. The new version successfully passed a comprehensive security audit and has now been officially released to end users. In parallel, “Clear Signin” for smart contract interactions was completed: all contract parameters are now displayed directly on-device, and for widely used token contracts the app shows highly readable messages such as “Transfer 100 USDC to XXXX,” which greatly improves user confidence and transaction safety.

On the Trezor side, full support for managing Stellar tokens has now shipped in Trezor Suite Desktop. Users can securely send, receive, and manage non-XLM assets directly within the official Trezor interface, significantly expanding Stellar’s usability and accessibility for everyday users.

Together, these improvements substantially strengthen the security, reliability, and usability of Stellar across the two most important hardware wallet platforms, lower barriers for both users and developers, and meaningfully increase overall ecosystem trust.
Deliverables Past 3 Months:
1. Ledger: Major Architecture and Security Upgrade
   This quarter, the Stellar Ledger App underwent a complete architectural transformation. The application was fully rewritten in Rust, resulting in reduced technical complexity, improved performance and user experience, and a more sustainable long-term codebase. The new implementation successfully passed a full security audit and has now been officially released.
   Release notes: https://github.com/LedgerHQ/app-stellar/blob/develop/release-notes.md#v600

2. Ledger: Clear Signing for Smart Contracts
   Clear Signing support for Soroban smart contracts is now fully implemented. All contract parameters are displayed directly on the hardware device, and for popular token contracts the app provides human-readable messages such as “Transfer 100 USDC to XXXX,” allowing users to clearly understand what they are signing and significantly improving transaction safety and user trust.
   Release notes: https://github.com/LedgerHQ/app-stellar/blob/develop/release-notes.md#v600

3. Ledger: Test Infrastructure Modernization
   The Ledger test suite has been fully migrated to Ledger’s official testing framework and expanded with additional test coverage, improving reliability, maintainability, and alignment with upstream development standards.
   Tests code: https://github.com/LedgerHQ/app-stellar/tree/develop/tests

4. Trezor: Full Stellar Token Support in Trezor Suite Desktop
   Stellar token functionality is now fully available in Trezor Suite Desktop. Users can securely send, receive, and manage Stellar non-native assets directly from the official Trezor interface.
   Release notes: https://trezor.io/other/product-updates/trezor-suite-updates/trezor-suite-update-december-2025
Goals Upcoming 3 Months: Over the next three months, I will continue strengthening Stellar’s hardware wallet ecosystem with a focus on performance optimization and expanded mobile support. The objective is to further improve scalability, accessibility, and long-term sustainability of Stellar’s hardware wallet infrastructure while maintaining strong security guarantees.
Upcoming Deliverables 3 month:
1. Ledger App Optimization and Size Reduction
   Over the next quarter, I will optimize the Stellar Ledger App with a focus on reducing its binary size (currently ~150 KB). This will improve performance, reliability, and leave more room for future protocol upgrades, ensuring the long-term sustainability of Stellar’s hardware wallet support.

2. Full Stellar Token Support in Trezor Suite Mobile
   I will extend full Stellar token support to Trezor Suite Mobile, enabling mobile users to securely send, receive, and manage non-XLM assets. This significantly improves everyday usability for mobile-first users and expands Stellar’s reach across platforms.

The Ledger app rewrite, clear signing for Soroban, and test infra modernization directly address stability, maintainability, functionality, and user security/usability. - Explore new features that improve contract usability and user security: Delivered.

Critical gaps vs original impact promises, as well as measurement gaps

- Coverage gaps: Unclear extent of clear-signing support across Soroban data types, contract methods, and edge cases; no statement on Trezor smart contract support or clear signing parity.

- Performance/QA metrics: No benchmarks, regression rates, test coverage %, fuzz/property testing, or hardware-in-the-loop results reported.

- Developer and wallet ecosystem enablement: Provide integration guides, example code, and changelogs; coordinate with major Stellar wallets to ensure timely adoption and consistent UX.
- Security UX enhancements: Consider policy prompts (amount limits, address allowlists), better error messaging, and recovery/failure-handling guidance to further reduce signing risk.

Retro Impact Past 3 Months: Over the last three months, maintenance and incremental improvements were delivered for the Java Stellar SDK. While my primary development focus this quarter was on hardware wallet infrastructure, I continued providing essential support for the Java SDK by implementing key protocol features, resolving reported issues, and ensuring compatibility with evolving Stellar standards. These efforts preserved the SDK’s stability, reliability, and long-term usability for developers building on Stellar.
Deliverables Past 3 Months:
1. Ongoing SDK Maintenance
   Continued routine maintenance work, including minor bug fixes and code improvements, which are reflected in the changelog.
   Relevant changes are included in: https://github.com/lightsail-network/java-stellar-sdk/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#220

2. Add Support for SEP-45
   Support for SEP-45 has been fully implemented and released, enabling contract-based Stellar Web Authentication in the Java SDK.
   Implementation details and release notes: https://github.com/lightsail-network/java-stellar-sdk/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#220

3. Responding to user requests and addressing possible issues.
   In the past quarter, we handled a small number of user inquiries in multiple channels.
Goals Upcoming 3 Months: In the next three months, I will continue maintaining and improving the SDK, with a focus on protocol updates, bug fixes, code quality, and test coverage to ensure its long-term stability.
Upcoming Deliverables 3 month:
1. Ongoing SDK Maintenance
   This deliverable covers all necessary improvements to ensure the SDK's long-term viability. It includes resolving defects, implementing new features, refining code quality, and increasing test coverage to guarantee the SDK remains a robust and dependable tool for developers.

2. Add Support for SEP-51
   I will add support for [SEP-51](https://github.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/blob/master/ecosystem/sep-0051.md), enabling developers to serialize XDR data into JSON. This simplifies integration with web services and tooling, improves developer experience, and promotes consistent behavior across applications in the Stellar ecosystem.

3. xdrgen Migration and Refactoring
   I am going to migrate xdrgen into a dedicated repository and perform necessary refactoring to improve maintainability and long-term sustainability of the SDK’s tooling. This will strengthen the build pipeline and make future protocol upgrades easier to support. ref: https://github.com/orgs/stellar/discussions/1738

4. Improve the scval build feature. 
   Currently, when building a map, we require users to manually sort the keys. I plan to enhance the scval build functionality to automatically sort the keys of the map, thereby simplifying the developer experience and reducing potential sources of errors.
   
5. Responding to User Requests and Addressing Possible Issues
   I will continue responding to user requests and addressing reported issues from the community, ensuring the SDK remains dependable and production-ready for developers.

Next 3 months plan vs prior 3 months plan (overlap and shifts):

Recommendations to improve submission (to realign with stated goals and maximize impact):

   We plan to migrate xdrgen into a dedicated repository and perform necessary refactoring to improve maintainability and long-term sustainability of the SDK’s tooling. This will strengthen the build pipeline and make future protocol upgrades easier to support.

4. Improve the scval build feature. 
   Currently, when building a map, we require users to manually sort the keys. I plan to enhance the scval build functionality to automatically sort the keys of the map, thereby simplifying the developer experience and reducing potential sources of errors. ref: https://github.com/orgs/stellar/discussions/1738

5. Responding to User Requests and Addressing Possible Issues
   I will continue responding to user requests and addressing reported issues from the community, ensuring the SDK remains dependable and production-ready for developers.

Goals Upcoming 3 Months:
The primary objectives for the upcoming quarter focus on maintaining SDK reliability, expanding Stellar Ecosystem Proposal (SEP) coverage, and enhancing XDR functionality:
- Continuous Compatibility: Ensure seamless integration with Horizon, Soroban RPC, and all SEP updates as they are released.
- Responsive Maintenance: Provide timely responses to GitHub issues, feature requests, and Discord inquiries to support the developer community.
- Technical Modernization: Refactor legacy code to align with current standards, enhance unit test coverage and documentation quality. Add token efficient documentation for AI coding agents and implement an XDR class generator.
Upcoming Deliverables 3 month:
1. Continuous Maintenance and improvement: Regular SDK updates addressing horizon, rpc, protocol updates (e.g. p25), bug fixes, feature requests (e.g. full web support), code modernization, unit test coverage improvement, and documentation enhancements to ensure long-term sustainability. This includes a new documentation for AI Coding Agents, a so called "skill", providing token efficient documentation and best practices when using the SDK. Furthermore it includes setting up a configuration for unit test coverage tracking and adding to CI/CD pipeline.  
2. SEP-53 Support:  SEP-53 standardizes message signing functionality across Stellar wallets, libraries, and services, preventing ecosystem fragmentation and ensuring interoperability. SEP-53 support is also implemented in the Java and Python SDKs.
3. XDR classes generator: Currently the SDK has approximatively 400 manually written XDR classes used to encode and decode XDR objects. Extensions or updates in the XDR structure need to be implemented manually at this time, which makes the SDK difficult to maintain. The new XDR class generator will improve the maintainability and also add missing XDR classes so that they can immediately be used as new features require them. For example, the v25.0.0 RPC getLatestLedger method also returns now the base64 encoded ledger close metadata XDR, that currently can not be decoded by the SDK.

Team Description (from Project):
My name is Christian (also known as Soneso in the Stellar Community) and I am the main developer and maintainer of the iOS Stellar SDK. I have a master's degree in computer science and a master's degree in logistics and have been working in the IT field since the mid-1990s as a developer, software architect, project manager, and in other roles.

I began contributing to the Stellar network in 2017, specializing primarily in the development and maintenance of Stellar SDKs. The first Stellar SDK I developed was the iOS Stellar SDK, which won the Stellar Build Challenge in 2018 and has since been expanded and maintained by me with the help of the SDF Infrastructure Grant. Other SDKs I have developed include the Flutter Stellar SDK, the PHP Stellar SDK, and the AssemblyScript Soroban SDK.

The iOS Stellar SDK also has many contributions from other developers, which can be viewed here. I currently work full-time on my Stellar SDK projects. If my capacity is insufficient during peak periods, I have an experienced contractor whom I can hire to assist me with development.

Goals Upcoming 3 Months:
The primary objectives for the upcoming three month focus on maintaining SDK reliability, expanding Stellar Ecosystem Proposal (SEP) coverage, and enhancing XDR functionality:
- Continuous Compatibility: Ensure seamless integration with Horizon, Soroban RPC, and all SEP updates as they are released.
- Responsive Maintenance: Provide timely responses to GitHub issues, feature requests, and Discord inquiries to support the developer community.
- Technical Modernization: Refactor legacy code to align with current standards, enhance unit test coverage and documentation quality. Add token efficient documentation for AI coding agents and implement an XDR class generator.
Upcoming Deliverables 3 month:
1. Continuous Maintenance and improvement: Regular SDK updates addressing horizon, rpc, protocol updates (e.g. p25), bug fixes, feature requests, code modernization, unit test coverage improvement, and documentation enhancements to ensure long-term sustainability. This includes a new documentation for AI Coding Agents, a so called "skill", providing token efficient documentation and best practices when using the SDK. Furthermore it includes setting up a configuration for unit test coverage tracking and adding to CI/CD pipeline.  
2. Add SEP-53 Support: SEP-53 standardizes message signing functionality across Stellar wallets, libraries, and services, preventing ecosystem fragmentation and ensuring interoperability. SEP-53 support is also implemented in the Java and Python SDKs.
3. XDR classes generator: Currently the SDK has hundreds of manually written XDR classes used to encode and decode XDR objects. Extensions or updates in the XDR structure need to be implemented manually at this time, which makes the SDK difficult to maintain. The new XDR class generator will improve the maintainability and also add missing XDR classes so that they can immediately be used as new features require them. For example, the v25.0.0 RPC getLatestLedger method also returns now the base64 encoded ledger close metadata XDR, that currently can not be decoded by the SDK.

Retro Impact Past 3 Months:
Over the last three months, the project delivered a substantial upgrade to the correctness, completeness, and production-readiness of the .NET Stellar SDK, significantly reducing integration risk for developers building on Stellar using C# and .NET.
Key areas of impact include:
- Protocol coverage expansion (SEPs): Implemented and improved support for multiple Stellar Ecosystem Proposals, including SEP-1, SEP-6, SEP-9, SEP-10, and SEP-24, enabling full anchor and authentication flows for deposits, withdrawals, KYC, and user authorization. This unblocked real-world anchor integrations without requiring custom SDK extensions.
- Correctness of Horizon response models: Fixed and improved a large set of response classes (e.g. Account, Ledger, Transaction, Trade, Path, Liquidity Pool, Claimable Balance, Trade Aggregation, Operation, Effect, Root and Transaction responses). These fixes addressed type inconsistencies, missing fields, incorrect nullability, and deserialization issues that previously caused subtle runtime bugs.
- Data type and consistency fixes: Resolved multiple inconsistencies in critical data types (prices, muxed account IDs, date/time fields), ensuring SDK models now accurately reflect Horizon and RPC responses and behave predictably in strongly typed .NET environments.
- Reliability improvements: Added a configurable retry mechanism for both Horizon Server and SorobanServer clients, improving resilience against transient network failures and rate limits in production systems.
- Soroban developer enablement: Added more advanced, real-world Soroban contract invocation examples, lowering the barrier for .NET developers adopting smart contracts on Stellar.
- Developer experience & safety: Reduced non-nullable warnings, improved public property documentation, and expanded unit test coverage, making the SDK safer, easier to use, and easier to maintain for downstream projects.

As a result, the SDK is now significantly closer to full protocol parity, safer for production use, and better aligned with real-world Stellar usage patterns. These improvements directly benefit the wider Stellar ecosystem by enabling .NET developers to build wallets, backends, anchors, and Soroban-enabled applications without maintaining forks or workarounds.

Revenue Model? (Detailed):
The project qualifies as a public good because it provides open, free, and neutral infrastructure that benefits the entire Stellar ecosystem without preferential treatment or paywalls. As the first and oldest Stellar explorer, it offers long-term continuity, transparency, and historical data relied on by developers, validators, businesses, researchers, and the community.

It is critical to the ecosystem because it enables trust, accountability, and accessibility: anyone can independently verify transactions, accounts, assets, and network behaviour. This visibility is foundational for security audits, debugging, compliance checks, and ecosystem education.

It is non-commercial because it is not designed to extract value from users or prioritise profit-driven features. Its primary purpose is to promote ecosystem health, reliability, and openness, rather than monetisation, thereby ensuring equal access for all participants.

Retro Impact Past 3 Months:
Over the last three months, Scaffold Stellar consolidated its role as the “front door” of Stellar’s developer journey: a convention-over-configuration toolkit that helps teams go from idea to proof-of-concept quickly, with less time spent re-configuring the same application plumbing and more time spent validating dApp ideas on Stellar.

Adoption signal without invasive tracking: We intentionally do not ship user trackers inside the open-source toolchain. Instead, we rely on public, verifiable ecosystem signals. Scaffold Stellar is now a common default starting point at Stellar builder events; in practice, most hackathon participants we interacted with used Scaffold Stellar to bootstrap projects faster. This includes usage across multiple hackathons and conference builder programs (Istanbul Blockchain Week, Boston Stellar Hackathon, Meridian 2025 Hackathon, DoraHacks “Scaffold Stellar” Hackathon, and Devconnect Argentina), where we provided hands-on support and observed repeated reuse of Scaffold Stellar as the baseline stack.

Concrete traction and distribution:
- The repository currently shows 47 stars and 32 forks (a directional signal rather than a full usage proxy, given that hackathon teams frequently clone or template without starring/forking).
- Scaffold Stellar has a dedicated onboarding website and documentation, and it has been listed in the official Stellar developer documentation, establishing it as an endorsed entry point for builders.

Ecosystem value created:
- Developer support and coaching at builder events: we provided direct support for teams using Scaffold Stellar at Stellar hackathons and conference programs, accelerating time-to-demo and helping new contributors become productive quickly.
- Onboarding and education improvements: we published an in-depth tutorial that reduces “time to first success,” and we produced additional video content to explain workflows and best practices.
- Reference implementations demonstrating frontier capability: we shipped and showcased a Noir ZK “Sudoku prover” example to prove Scaffold Stellar can support ambitious, non-trivial applications beyond basic demos. It was the first known ZK application on Stellar.
- Showcase and real project bootstraps: we expanded the Scaffold Showcase and supported multiple projects using Scaffold as a starting point, including NFT proof-of-concepts and the Stellar Merch Shop admin panel.
- Maintenance and UX maturity: we kept templates aligned with current best practices (including OpenZeppelin-based contract templates) and iterated on frontend design with the SDF design team.
- Registry readiness and trust foundations: we completed a security audit for the Stellar Registry workstream and advanced governance readiness (security council + mainnet readiness), supporting the longer-term goal of safer contract discoverability and reuse.

Measurement approach going forward: In the next cycle we will report adoption through non-invasive signals such as: number of hackathon submissions referencing Scaffold Stellar, GitHub traffic/clones (platform-provided), documentation consumption (site/server-level aggregates), and community contribution metrics (issues/PRs/extensions published), without embedding trackers into the codebase.

Goals Upcoming 3 Months:
Scaffold Stellar has proven it can materially accelerate the first steps of a Soroban project: developers spend less time re-configuring the same plumbing and more time proving out a dApp idea. Over the next three months, our goal is to evolve Scaffold Stellar from a “quickstart toolkit” into a production-oriented developer platform, addressing the concrete gaps teams hit when moving from hackathon proof-of-concept to sustainable deployment.

We will focus on three impact objectives:

A. Make Soroban contract reuse safe, discoverable, and production-grade via the Stellar Contract Registry launch
Following the successful security audit of the contract registry (https://sorobansecurity.com/report/63), we will move into an official launch phase. The goal is to deliver an experience for Soroban contracts analogous to npm / crates.io / Anaconda: a place where developers can reliably discover, evaluate, and consume standard contracts and ecosystem primitives with clear provenance and safe defaults.

B. Turn Scaffold Stellar into an ecosystem distribution surface through an extension/plugin system
We will ship an extension system so wallets, indexers, and ecosystem services can plug into Scaffold Stellar without forking the core project. The goal is to make Scaffold Stellar an “integration hub” that amplifies other public goods: wallet standards, indexing providers, and key DeFi APIs can become one-click options at project creation time.

C. Reduce time-to-production through iterative DX improvements validated in the field
We will rework core components based on observed friction from real builders (particularly hackathon teams), improve project creation UX, add quality-of-life commands, and strengthen “import and adapt” workflows so teams can adopt existing contracts rather than starting from scratch.

Developer adoption and demand are already visible without invasive tracking. We deliberately do not embed trackers into the open-source toolchain because we believe privacy-respecting infrastructure should not require code-level trackers. Instead, we rely on verifiable ecosystem signals and direct builder workflows.

Evidence of continued usage and relevance includes:
- Observed default usage at builder events: In practice, Scaffold Stellar has become the common bootstrapping path for many hackathon participants building on Soroban. We have supported and observed teams using Scaffold Stellar across multiple hackathons and conference programs, including Istanbul Blockchain Week, Boston Stellar Hackathon, Meridian 2025 Hackathon, the DoraHacks “Scaffold Stellar” hackathon, and Devconnect Argentina.
- Official ecosystem distribution: Scaffold Stellar is listed in the official Stellar developer documentation, establishing it as an endorsed entry point for developers: https://developers.stellar.org/docs/tools/scaffold-stellar
- It is also supported by a dedicated onboarding site: https://scaffoldstellar.org/
- Public repository signals: the repository currently has 47 stars and 32 forks. We recognize this is not a complete proxy for usage (many teams clone/instantiate without starring or forking), but it is a public indicator of awareness and developer engagement.

Ecosystem benefits over the next three months will be tangible and compounding:
- Higher builder throughput and conversion: better defaults and production-oriented workflows mean more hackathon projects can become maintained testnet deployments and eventually mainnet applications.
- Safer contract reuse and less fragmentation: a registry with namespaces, governance, and supply-chain protections reduces copy/paste proliferation and encourages reusable, standard building blocks.
- Amplification of other public goods: the extension system allows wallets, indexers, and ecosystem services to reach developers at the moment they start new projects, improving adoption of best-in-class infrastructure without centralizing control.
- Reduced reset pain and higher reliability: deterministic redeploy and reseeding tooling around testnet/futurenet resets removes recurring ecosystem friction and helps developers build confidently through network lifecycle events.

Team Description:
Scaffold Stellar is built and maintained by The Aha Company (formerly Aha Labs), a team of 10+ senior engineers deeply embedded in the Stellar ecosystem.

Early Soroban origin:
In 2022 (before Soroban had a name) SDF already had a clear ambition: launch their upcoming smart contract platform with a “batteries-included” developer experience. The gap was execution capacity: there was no in-house team available to design and implement the developer workflows needed to make that promise real. Tyler van der Hoeven went to major blockchain conferences to find the right team, and identified The Aha Company as the team with the right combination of product mindset and deep technical ability to “install the batteries.”

Foundational Stellar developer workflows we designed and shipped:
We envisioned, architected, and implemented several of the workflows that have become core to Soroban development on Stellar, including:
- Stellar CLI smart contract workflows, such as the contract invoke behavior and associated developer ergonomics that simplify testing, deployment, and interaction.
- JavaScript developer experience patterns, including the Contract Client behavior in stellar-sdk-js, which helps application developers interact with contracts more safely and predictably.

Why we were selected for Scaffold Stellar and our SCF track record:
In early 2025, SDF searched for a team that could bring a ScaffoldETH-like end-to-end experience to Stellar. They selected us based on:
1. our deep CLI & JS expertise proven through shipped core tooling, and
2. our track record delivering developer infrastructure via SCF, including:
    - Smart Deploy: https://communityfund.stellar.org/project/smart-deploy-yoj
    - Loam: https://communityfund.stellar.org/project/loam-qj5

Scaffold Stellar is a direct continuation of that work: turning the hard-won Developer Experience (DevX) knowledge from core tooling into a “front door” experience that helps developers go from idea to proof-of-concept quickly, with strong defaults and a convention-over-configuration approach.

Ongoing maintenance and production-grade integration experience:
Since then, we have remained engaged with SDF to support and maintain key tooling (most recently improving Stellar CLI handling of hardware-based keys) and we continue to operate as an integration partner on production deployments. Notably, we architected and developed Société Générale’s EURCV on Stellar (live soon), bringing a rigorous, real-world perspective to developer tooling and reliability requirements.

Deep community participation and ecosystem leadership:
Our team includes well-known ecosystem contributors. Several members hold key community roles (e.g., SCF Pilot, category delegates) and actively build their own SCF projects (e.g., Moonlight, Tansu, Stellar Merch Store). We contribute to protocol and tooling discussions, provide developer support at hackathons and conferences, and invest heavily in community outreach and education. We show up consistently at major events and actively communicate about Stellar, both its strengths and the practical realities builders need to know.

Cross-ecosystem perspective (DevX benchmarking):
Beyond Stellar, The Aha Company is also an integration partner in other ecosystems (e.g., Filecoin, XRPL, ICP). This gives us a unique ability to benchmark developer experience across chains and bring proven patterns back to Stellar—while keeping Scaffold Stellar aligned with what developers expect from modern, full-stack tooling.
Revenue Model? (Detailed):
Scaffold Stellar is the front door of Stellar’s developer journey. At hackathons and conferences, we repeatedly see the same pattern: new contributors have strong ideas, but lose critical time re-configuring the same application plumbing (project structure, wallet wiring, contract setup, scripts, and frontend defaults) before they can even demonstrate a proof-of-concept. Scaffold Stellar flips that: less time on repetitive configuration; more time proving out the dApp idea and validating it on-chain.

This is not just a learning tool. It is a foundation for a convention-over-configuration approach to building ambitious dApps with Stellar at their core. By standardizing the “first 80%” of a Soroban application, Scaffold Stellar reduces the fixed cost of shipping on Stellar, increases developer throughput, and improves the consistency and maintainability of new projects across the ecosystem.

It qualifies as a public good because it is critical infrastructure for developer adoption and is non-commercial by design:
- Open source and freely available: everything we build ships in public repos with documentation anyone can use, fork, or contribute to.
- Collaborating rather than competing: we work with existing ecosystem projects (wallets, indexers, developer tools, protocols) to incorporate their offerings, so teams can start from best-in-class components instead of reinventing integrations.
- Compounding ecosystem impact: every new project that starts from Scaffold Stellar inherits shared defaults and best practices, which reduces fragmentation and improves long-term sustainability for the network.
- Community-driven momentum: after hackathons we consistently receive strong community feedback, and we have begun onboarding new contributors directly into the Scaffold Stellar contributor pipeline.

Finally, we are doubling down on ecosystem interoperability: this quarter we will ship an extension system that makes it straightforward for ecosystem projects to plug into Scaffold Stellar without forks. This turns Scaffold Stellar into a scalable distribution surface for Stellar’s broader developer tooling landscape, amplifying the impact of other public goods, not competing with them.

Team Description:

Revenue Model? (Detailed):
SoroPG lowers the barrier to entry for new developers coming to Stellar. I can show someone how to deploy their first smart contract in less than 5 minutes and all they need is a web browser. It's also a playground for experienced contract developers to experiment and test ideas in Soroban.

The project in non-extractive and has no token, paywall or subscription charges. Developers will not pay for tooling that they can use for free in other competing ecosystems.

Today SoroPG is the only functioning web based IDE for Stellar smart contracts, it's been self-funded since launch in May 2025. The site has been well maintained and dependencies are updated in a timely manner to ensure the best developer experience.

Public goods funding can ensure long-term sustainability, neutrality and availability of critical developer infrastructure on Stellar.
